name: PG&E Smart Meter Fires
description: The PG&E Smart Meter Fires controversy revolves around claims that the installation of smart meters by Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E) in California led to an increase in house fires. Proponents of this theory argue that the radio frequency (RF) radiation emitted by the meters, faulty installation, or inherent design flaws caused the fires. They often point to anecdotal evidence, clusters of fires occurring shortly after smart meter installations, and reports of meter malfunctions. Some also suggest that PG&E deliberately downplayed or covered up the issue to avoid liability and maintain public trust. Mainstream perspectives, including investigations by the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) and independent engineering firms, have largely refuted the direct causal link between smart meters and fires. These investigations typically attribute fires near smart meters to pre-existing electrical problems in homes, improper wiring, or unrelated equipment failures. They emphasize that smart meters are designed to meet safety standards and undergo rigorous testing. While acknowledging isolated incidents of meter malfunctions, these reports conclude that the overall risk of fire from smart meters is low and comparable to traditional analog meters. The debate continues, fueled by distrust of large corporations and concerns about the potential health effects of RF radiation, despite scientific consensus generally finding low-level RF exposure from smart meters to be safe.
subtitle: Smart Meters and Alleged Fire Risk

claims.claim: Smart meters emit harmful RF radiation that ignites fires.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of fires occurring shortly after smart meter installation; claims of RF radiation causing overheating.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science asserts that RF radiation from smart meters is low-level and insufficient to cause ignition; independent investigations attribute fires to pre-existing electrical issues.

claims.claim: PG&E is covering up evidence of smart meter-related fires.
claims.evidence: Allegations of PG&E downplaying incidents, suppressing negative reports, and manipulating data.
claims.criticism: CPUC investigations and independent audits have not found evidence of widespread cover-ups, although some acknowledge instances of miscommunication or delayed responses.

claims.claim: Faulty smart meter installation is a primary cause of fires.
claims.evidence: Reports of improperly installed meters, loose wiring, and inadequate grounding.
claims.criticism: While improper installation can contribute to electrical hazards, investigations suggest it's not a widespread issue and pre-existing electrical problems are more frequently the root cause.

keyFigures.name: Theodora Scarato
keyFigures.role: Executive Director, Environmental Health Trust, advocating for safer technology and raising concerns about smart meter safety.

keyFigures.name: California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC)
keyFigures.role: Regulator conducting investigations and commissioning reports on smart meter safety.

keyFigures.name: Exponent
keyFigures.role: Engineering and Scientific Consulting firm hired by PG&E and others to investigate claims.

timeline.event: PG&E begins smart meter deployment in California.
Reports of house fires allegedly linked to smart meters increase, sparking public concern.
CPUC and independent investigations are conducted to assess the safety of smart meters.
Debate and legal challenges continue regarding smart meter safety and potential health effects.
